title: What are the differences between Comparable and Comparator interfaces, and when would you use each?

answers.junior: Comparable and Comparator are both interfaces used for sorting objects in Java, but they serve different purposes and are used in different contexts. Comparable (in java.lang) defines the natural ordering of a class by implementing the compareTo(T o) method within the class itself, making it the default sort order — for example, class Employee implements Comparable<Employee> { public int compareTo(Employee other) { return this.name.compareTo(other.name); } }.
answers.middle: Comparable and Comparator are both interfaces used for sorting objects in Java, but they serve different purposes and are used in different contexts. Comparable (in java.lang) defines the natural ordering of a class by implementing the compareTo(T o) method within the class itself, making it the default sort order — for example, class Employee implements Comparable<Employee> { public int compareTo(Employee other) { return this.name.compareTo(other.name); } }. Comparator (in java.util) is an external strategy for comparison, allowing multiple sort orders without modifying the original class — for example, Comparator<Employee> bySalary = Comparator.comparingDouble(Employee::getSalary);. Use Comparable when there is one obvious, natural ordering for the class (e.g., String, Integer, Date all implement it), and you control the class source code. Use Comparator when you need multiple different orderings, cannot modify the class (third-party or legacy), or need context-specific sorting at runtime.
answers.senior: Comparable and Comparator are both interfaces used for sorting objects in Java, but they serve different purposes and are used in different contexts. Comparable (in java.lang) defines the natural ordering of a class by implementing the compareTo(T o) method within the class itself, making it the default sort order — for example, class Employee implements Comparable<Employee> { public int compareTo(Employee other) { return this.name.compareTo(other.name); } }. Comparator (in java.util) is an external strategy for comparison, allowing multiple sort orders without modifying the original class — for example, Comparator<Employee> bySalary = Comparator.comparingDouble(Employee::getSalary);. Use Comparable when there is one obvious, natural ordering for the class (e.g., String, Integer, Date all implement it), and you control the class source code. Use Comparator when you need multiple different orderings, cannot modify the class (third-party or legacy), or need context-specific sorting at runtime. Collections.sort(list) uses Comparable, while Collections.sort(list, comparator) uses Comparator. Java 8 enhanced Comparator significantly with default and static methods like comparing(), thenComparing(), reversed(), and naturalOrder(), enabling fluent, composable comparators: Comparator<Employee> comp = Comparator.comparing(Employee::getDepartment).thenComparingDouble(Employee::getSalary).reversed();. The compareTo/compare contract requires consistency: returning negative, zero, or positive integer to indicate less-than, equal, or greater-than. A critical pitfall is using subtraction for integer comparison (return a - b) which can overflow for extreme values; always use Integer.compare(a, b) instead.
answers.tricky: Comparable and Comparator are both interfaces used for sorting objects in Java, but they serve different purposes and are used in different contexts. Comparable (in java.lang) defines the natural ordering of a class by implementing the compareTo(T o) method within the class itself, making it the default sort order — for example, class Employee implements Comparable<Employee> { public int compareTo(Employee other) { return this.name.compareTo(other.name); } }. Comparator (in java.util) is an external strategy for comparison, allowing multiple sort orders without modifying the original class — for example, Comparator<Employee> bySalary = Comparator.comparingDouble(Employee::getSalary);. Use Comparable when there is one obvious, natural ordering for the class (e.g., String, Integer, Date all implement it), and you control the class source code. Use Comparator when you need multiple different orderings, cannot modify the class (third-party or legacy), or need context-specific sorting at runtime. Collections.sort(list) uses Comparable, while Collections.sort(list, comparator) uses Comparator. Java 8 enhanced Comparator significantly with default and static methods like comparing(), thenComparing(), reversed(), and naturalOrder(), enabling fluent, composable comparators: Comparator<Employee> comp = Comparator.comparing(Employee::getDepartment).thenComparingDouble(Employee::getSalary).reversed();. The compareTo/compare contract requires consistency: returning negative, zero, or positive integer to indicate less-than, equal, or greater-than. A critical pitfall is using subtraction for integer comparison (return a - b) which can overflow for extreme values; always use Integer.compare(a, b) instead. In production, Comparator.nullsFirst() and nullsLast() handle null-safety elegantly without manual null checks.
